title: Biostatistics for Clinical Research: A Journalist's Guide
description: Learn to understand and critically evaluate health research through the lens of investigative journalism

nodes.title: Understanding Data in Health Journalism
nodes.content: When journalists report on health stories—from hospital quality rankings to poll results on public health policy—they must understand the data they're presenting. This module explores the types of data health reporters encounter: categorical data (like hospital accreditation status: yes/no), ordinal data (quality rankings: 1-5 stars), and numerical data (patient age, test scores). You'll learn how to describe data distributions using mean, median, and standard deviation—the fundamental tools for reporting accurately on health statistics. These descriptive statistics form the foundation for all health journalism reporting.

nodes.title: Analyzing Hospital Mortality Rates Across States
nodes.content: Imagine you're a health reporter investigating hospital safety. The CDC provides mortality rate data (5-year average deaths per 1,000 patients) for 4,800 hospitals. For your article comparing Midwest hospitals, you calculate: mean mortality rate = 8.2%, median = 7.9%, and standard deviation = 2.1%. The low standard deviation tells you most Midwest hospitals cluster near the average—a key insight for your story. However, you notice three hospitals with mortality rates >15%, suggesting outliers worth investigating further. This is descriptive statistics in action: summarizing complex data into meaningful numbers that tell your readers something true about hospital safety.

nodes.title: Quiz: Interpreting Descriptive Statistics
nodes.content: You've reviewed hospital data for your story. Test your understanding of how descriptive statistics work in health journalism.

questions.question: You're writing about patient satisfaction across 100 clinics. The mean satisfaction score is 7.8/10, but the standard deviation is 3.1. What does this tell you?
options: Patient satisfaction is universally high across all clinics
There's high variability in patient satisfaction—some clinics score 9/10 while others score 5/10 or lower
The data is unreliable and shouldn't be reported
Patient satisfaction is the same at each clinic
feedback: Incorrect. A high standard deviation indicates variability, not universally high scores.
Correct! High standard deviation means significant variation. This is crucial for your reporting—it tells readers there's no one-size-fits-all story.
Incorrect. Standard deviation is a valid and important statistical measure.
Incorrect. If all clinics had the same score, the standard deviation would be 0.
questions.explanation: Understanding variability (standard deviation) is essential for accurate health journalism. It prevents oversimplifying complex data into misleading headlines.

questions.question: You're analyzing survey data on Americans' trust in health institutions. What data type are you working with if respondents answer: 'Very Distrustful,' 'Somewhat Distrustful,' 'Neutral,' 'Somewhat Trustful,' 'Very Trustful'?
options: Nominal data (categories with no order)
Ordinal data (ordered categories)
Numerical/continuous data
Binary data
feedback: Incorrect. Nominal data has no order, but these responses have a clear ranking from distrusting to trusting.
Correct! Ordinal data has meaningful order but no fixed intervals. This distinction matters for which statistics you can report.
Incorrect. These are categories, not numerical measurements like age or blood pressure.
Incorrect. Binary data has only two options, but this scale has five levels.
questions.explanation: Recognizing data types helps you choose appropriate statistics to report and avoid misinterpreting your findings for your health story.

nodes.title: Evaluating Research and Statistical Significance
nodes.content: After years reporting health stories, you'll eventually encounter published research claiming breakthrough findings. This is where inferential statistics matters: How do researchers draw conclusions from a sample to make claims about a larger population? Journalists must understand hypothesis testing (Did this drug really work better than placebo?), p-values (How unlikely is this result if there's truly no effect?), confidence intervals (What range of effects could this treatment actually have?), and study design flaws (Was the sample biased? Were there confounding variables?). These tools separate credible research from overhyped marketing disguised as science—critical skills for responsible health journalism.

nodes.title: Investigating a Viral Study on a New Arthritis Treatment
nodes.content: Your news outlet receives a press release about a 'miracle arthritis treatment' showing 85% improvement rates. Before writing the headline, you investigate: The study had only 40 patients (small sample), used a company-selected patient group (selection bias), lasted only 8 weeks (short-term), and reported p=0.08 (not statistically significant at the standard p<0.05 threshold). Moreover, 30% of participants were company employees, and there was no control group for comparison. Your investigation reveals multiple red flags: the 'miracle' result could easily be a fluke, and the sample isn't representative. A responsible journalist would either skip this story or report it as 'promising early data requiring further research'—not a breakthrough.

nodes.title: Quiz: Evaluating Clinical Research
nodes.content: You've received several research studies to report on. Can you identify which are credible and which have statistical or design problems?

questions.question: A study of 2,000 patients over 2 years finds that Drug X reduced heart attack risk with p=0.02 and 95% confidence interval [0.15, 0.35] for risk reduction. As a journalist, what can you responsibly report?
options: 'Drug X cures heart attacks'
'Drug X is completely ineffective'
'Drug X appears to reduce heart attack risk by roughly 15-35%, a statistically significant finding'
'The results are too uncertain to report anything'
feedback: Incorrect. Overstating results ('cures') is irresponsible journalism and misleads readers.
Incorrect. The p-value shows statistical significance, not ineffectiveness.
Correct! You've accurately reported the size of the effect and its statistical significance while avoiding overstatement.
Incorrect. The p-value and confidence interval show meaningful, reportable findings—just not certainty.
questions.explanation: Responsible health journalism reports findings accurately: noting both the size of effects and their statistical significance. Avoid 'cure' language and always convey appropriate uncertainty.

questions.question: A study claims a dietary supplement prevents Alzheimer's disease. You discover: 100 people took the supplement, no control group used a placebo, researchers are investors in the supplement company, and results haven't been published in a peer-reviewed journal—just presented at a company conference. What's your reporting recommendation?
options: Run a prominent story: 'Scientists Discover Alzheimer's Prevention'
Decline to cover it due to multiple conflicts of interest and lack of peer review
Report it as breaking news but note the company's involvement
Wait for larger studies before mentioning it
feedback: Incorrect. Multiple red flags suggest this isn't credible science: no control group, conflicts of interest, no peer review.
Correct! The lack of a control group makes results uninterpretable, conflicts of interest suggest bias, and lack of peer review means unvetted claims.
Incorrect. 'Breaking news' language overstates findings that haven't met peer-review standards.
Incorrect. You shouldn't report unvetted claims in health journalism, even 'noted' ones.
questions.explanation: Study design flaws (missing control groups, conflicts of interest, lack of peer review) are red flags suggesting results aren't credible. Strong journalists reject such stories to maintain public trust.

nodes.title: Integrated Practice: From Data to Journalism
nodes.content: You're now working as an investigative health reporter. These scenarios combine descriptive and inferential statistics skills you need in real reporting.

questions.question: The CDC reports COVID-19 hospitalization rates: mean age of hospitalized patients = 68 years, standard deviation = 12 years. A news outlet headlines: 'COVID Primarily Affects the Elderly.' You review the data and find it includes pediatric cases (some patients are under 18). What's the statistical issue with this headline?
options: The mean age is misleading when the data includes extreme outliers like children and very elderly patients
The standard deviation is too large to report
COVID doesn't actually affect age groups differently
The sample size is too small
feedback: Correct! A bimodal distribution (separate peaks for elderly and children) creates a misleading mean. The median or stratified reporting would be more accurate.
Incorrect. Standard deviation isn't 'too large'—it conveys meaningful variability.
Incorrect. The data shows real age differences; the issue is how to report them accurately.
Incorrect. The sample size isn't the problem here.
questions.explanation: Sometimes descriptive statistics like mean can hide important patterns, especially when data is bimodal. Always consider whether your chosen statistic actually represents the story you're telling.

questions.question: You're vetting a study claiming Exercise Program X reduces depression. The study: randomly assigned 300 people to exercise or control condition, lasted 12 weeks, found p=0.001 (highly significant). However, you discover 60% of exercise group participants dropped out, while 20% of control group did. What concern does this raise?
options: The p-value is too small to be real
The dropout difference suggests selection bias—perhaps those who benefited most stuck with the program, inflating the effect
Exercise programs don't actually work
The sample size is too large
feedback: Incorrect. A p-value of 0.001 is very strong evidence—the issue isn't the p-value itself.
Correct! Differential dropout (60% vs 20%) introduces selection bias. Those who felt better may have stayed, inflating the apparent benefit.
Incorrect. The issue isn't whether exercise works, but whether this study design can prove it.
Incorrect. Larger samples are generally better; this isn't a statistical concern.
questions.explanation: Study design matters as much as p-values. Dropout patterns, selection bias, and study design flaws can make statistically significant results unreliable for reporting. Always dig into the methodology, not just the statistics.

nodes.title: You've Mastered Descriptive Statistics!
nodes.content: Congratulations—you now understand how to describe and interpret health data, a crucial foundation for any health journalist. Your next choice: Do you want to advance to inferential statistics (how to evaluate research claims and statistical significance), or would you prefer more practice on descriptive statistics before moving forward?

choices.label: Get more practice on descriptive statistics

nodes.title: Course Complete: You're Ready for Health Journalism
nodes.content: You've completed 'Biostatistics for Clinical Research: A Journalist's Guide.' You now understand how to describe health data accurately, identify statistical red flags in research, and report complex findings responsibly to the public. The next time you receive a study showing a 'breakthrough finding,' you'll know what questions to ask: Is the sample representative? Is the result statistically significant? Could confounding variables explain this? Are there conflicts of interest? These skills will make you a more credible, trustworthy health journalist. Keep asking tough statistical questions—your readers depend on it.
